players.name: Gatito Fernández
players.bio: At 38, 'Gatito' (Little Cat) is the veteran guardian of La Albirroja's goal. The Cerro Porteño shotstopper brings bags of big-game experience to his first World Cup, proving age is just a number.
players.facts: His nickname 'Gatito' means 'little kitten' — yet opposing strikers find him anything but cuddly.

players.name: Gustavo Velázquez
players.bio: Gustavo Velázquez is a reliable defensive presence for Cerro Porteño and Paraguay, chipping in with a rare international goal across 13 caps. A solid, no-nonsense full-back who keeps things simple and effective.
players.facts: Scored an international goal as a defender — more than some forwards ever manage for their country.

players.name: Omar Alderete
players.bio: Omar Alderete is a commanding centre-back who has built his career across European football before landing at Sunderland. Composed on the ball with 36 caps and 3 international goals, he's a set-piece threat from the back.
players.facts: Alderete plays for Sunderland — a club that once went 10 years without top-flight football. Redemption arcs suit him fine.

players.name: Juan José Cáceres
players.bio: Born in Isla Maciel, Argentina, Cáceres chose Paraguay and hasn't looked back. The young right-back joined Dynamo Moscow and brings youthful energy and attacking intent to this his first World Cup at just 26.
players.facts: Plays club football in Russia for Dynamo Moscow — one of the more geographically adventurous careers in this squad.

players.name: Fabián Balbuena
players.bio: A rock from Ciudad del Este, Fabián Balbuena has been a Premier League and South American stalwart. The Grêmio centre-back brings nearly 50 caps of nous and physicality to anchor Paraguay's defence at 34.
players.facts: Balbuena played in the Premier League for West Ham and Nottingham Forest — not bad for a lad from Ciudad del Este.

players.name: Júnior Alonso
players.bio: Júnior Alonso is Paraguay's most experienced defender on show, with 71 caps earned at clubs across South America and Brazil. The Atlético Mineiro stopper is a fierce competitor who leads by example at the back.
players.facts: With 71 caps, Alonso has faced more international opponents than most Paraguayans ever will — and stopped most of them.

players.name: Ramón Sosa
players.bio: Ramón Sosa is a tricky, direct winger who earned a big move to Palmeiras after dazzling in South America. Born in Maracaná, Paraguay — not the famous stadium — the 26-year-old brings flair and unpredictability.
players.facts: He's from a town called Maracaná in Paraguay — no pressure living up to that name on the world stage.

players.name: Diego Gómez
players.bio: Diego Gómez is one of the most exciting young midfielders in South America, earning a move to Brighton at just 20. Technically gifted with an eye for goal, the 23-year-old is Paraguay's brightest prospect.
players.facts: Gómez joined Brighton & Hove Albion — a club known for developing talent with data. He fits the profile perfectly.

players.name: Antonio Sanabria
players.bio: Antonio Sanabria has been Paraguay's go-to striker for years, earning 48 caps and 7 goals. Born in San Lorenzo, he's played across Europe and now leads the line at Cremonese with hold-up play and poacher's instinct.
players.facts: Sanabria once went viral for spitting toward a referee — a moment he'd probably like to leave off the highlights reel.

players.name: Miguel Almirón
players.bio: Miguel Almirón is Paraguay's most recognisable star — the creative engine whose energy and work-rate made him a Premier League fan favourite at Newcastle. Now back in MLS, the No.10 leads by example at 32.
players.facts: Newcastle fans loved him so much they invented 'Almiron's better than Messi' — peak terrace delusion, peak affection.

players.name: Maurício
players.bio: Born in São Paulo, Maurício elected to represent Paraguay and is still finding his feet internationally with just 3 caps. The Palmeiras defender brings Brazilian club-football pedigree to La Albirroja's backline.
players.facts: Born in Brazil, plays for Paraguay — one of football's great border-crossing career moves with only 3 caps to his name so far.

players.name: Orlando Gill
players.bio: Orlando Gill is the backup goalkeeper from San Lorenzo, Paraguay, who plays for his hometown club. With just 6 caps, this World Cup represents a huge opportunity for the 26-year-old shot-stopper to shine.
players.facts: Gill plays for San Lorenzo — the club sharing a name with his birthplace. Clearly never strayed too far from home.

players.name: José Canale
players.bio: José Canale is a dependable centre-back who has quietly built his career at Lanús in Argentina. With just 2 caps to his name, the 29-year-old from Itauguá arrives at his first World Cup as an unfashionable but reliable option.
players.facts: Two caps before a World Cup — Canale is proof that opportunity can arrive fashionably late but still knock loudly.

players.name: Andrés Cubas
players.bio: Andrés Cubas is a tenacious defensive midfielder born in Argentina who chose Paraguay and never looked back. Now anchoring midfield for Vancouver Whitecaps, the 30-year-old's 33 caps speak to consistent international trust.
players.facts: Born in Argentina, plays in MLS for Vancouver — Cubas's career has taken him further north than most Paraguayans dare.

players.name: Gustavo Gómez
players.bio: Gustavo Gómez is Paraguay's defensive cornerstone with 89 caps — one of the most decorated defenders in Albirroja history. The Palmeiras captain brings South American silverware experience and leadership to the World Cup.
players.facts: With 89 caps, Gómez is closing in on Paraguay's all-time appearance record — a one-man wall in green and white.

players.name: Damián Bobadilla
players.bio: Damián Bobadilla is a energetic midfielder who has earned his place at São Paulo with disciplined, industrious performances. Still only 24, his 19 caps suggest a player rapidly establishing himself as a key midfield cog.
players.facts: At 24 with 19 caps already, Bobadilla is on a trajectory that suggests his best football is still very much to come.

players.name: Kaku
players.bio: Kaku — real name Héctor Villalba — is a nippy, skilful forward with a knack for the spectacular. The Al Ain attacker has 6 international goals from 33 caps and brings creative unpredictability to Paraguay's attack.
players.facts: His nickname 'Kaku' stuck so completely that most fans don't even know his real name is Héctor Villalba.

players.name: Álex Arce
players.bio: Álex Arce is a tenacious forward who has grinded his way to 15 caps and a World Cup spot. Playing in Argentina's lower reaches of the top flight, his hunger and work-rate make him a useful option off the bench.
players.facts: Arce plays for Independiente Rivadavia — a club that finally reached Argentina's top flight after decades of waiting.

players.name: Julio Enciso
players.bio: Julio Enciso is Paraguay's wonderkid — a fearless, technically brilliant forward who once scored a stunning long-range screamer against Manchester City. Now 22 and at Strasbourg, the future belongs to him.
players.facts: Enciso once scored an absolute thunderbolt against Man City from outside the box. Even Pep Guardiola applauded.

players.name: Braian Ojeda
players.bio: Braian Ojeda is a combative, box-to-box midfielder who swapped Paraguayan football for MLS with Orlando City. His 17 caps reflect a player building quietly but steadily toward becoming a midfield mainstay.
players.facts: Ojeda plays in Orlando — home of theme parks and sunshine. His midfield work-rate suggests he doesn't take many days off.

players.name: Gabriel Ávalos
players.bio: Gabriel Ávalos is a experienced forward who has spent much of his career in Argentina, earning 23 caps for Paraguay. At 34, this World Cup may be his last hurrah — and he'll be determined to make it count.
players.facts: At 34, Ávalos is one of the oldest outfield players in the squad — seasoned, savvy, and still scoring at the top level.

players.name: Gastón Olveira
players.bio: Gastón Olveira is the third-choice goalkeeper at this World Cup, a veteran at 33 with just one cap to his name. The Olimpia stopper is a trusted figure domestically, even if international appearances have been rare.
players.facts: One cap in 33 years of life — Olveira has waited patiently longer than most for his international moment.

players.name: Matías Galarza
players.bio: Matías Galarza is a dynamic young midfielder with an impressive eye for goal — 3 strikes in 15 caps is a fine return. The Atlanta United man brings energy and ambition to Paraguay's midfield at just 24.
players.facts: Galarza and Almirón are Atlanta United teammates — Paraguay's World Cup pipeline apparently runs through Georgia.

players.name: Gustavo Caballero
players.bio: Gustavo Caballero is a young midfielder with a stunning return of 1 goal in just 2 caps. The Portsmouth man is one of Paraguay's most intriguing prospects, bringing English football's grit to La Albirroja's engine room.
players.facts: One goal in two caps as a midfielder — Caballero's scoring rate puts most strikers to shame. Statistically, at least.

players.name: Isidro Pitta
players.bio: Isidro Pitta is a powerful, physically imposing forward who offers Paraguay a different dimension up front. The Red Bull Bragantino man has 5 caps and brings pace and directness as an impact option from the bench.
players.facts: His surname 'Pitta' means a type of flatbread in some cultures — though defenders find him anything but soft.

players.name: Alexandro Maidana
players.bio: At just 20, Alexandro Maidana is the youngest player in Paraguay's squad — a defender at Talleres who has already scored in international football across 2 caps. One for the future, arriving early at his first World Cup.
players.facts: Aged 20 with a World Cup cap and an international goal — Maidana's career is off to a pace most veterans can only envy.
